speaker: interviewer
text: Bronia Sonnenschein, tape number two. The camera's rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mrs. Sonnenschein, your father is in Poland. Your mother and you and your sister are in Vienna.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you have to wait until you sneaked out of Vienna and got to be together with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was either January or February 1940. I'm not quite sure of the dates. My father spent a lot of money to have-- pay somebody to meet me at the border. A great problem was that I did not speak Polish because Poland and Germany were not getting along. Anyways, no. My father had warned me before it all happened, if I ever get there, I can't even open my mouth. I have to pretend I'm a deaf mute. That was very difficult. I was very lively and outspoken always. I was so petrified I probably wouldn't have spoken anyway. But I came to that point that I was supposed to meet that person. That person was there but, as we say in English, he got cold feet and he left me. I had a little suitcase. It was winter. I wore boots. I had no idea whether to turn to my right, to my left, or go straight ahead. I had no ideas. Obviously, a place I'd never been in my life. My miracle started maybe right then because I took the right way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me interrupt you just for a minute. Between your father's departure from Vienna and your arrival in-- in Poland, what was the span of time? How long it was before you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, it was quite a few months. Maybe even eight. It was quite a while. My father left, actually-- no. It was more. He left shortly after Kristallnacht, so it was still '38, '39 maybe beginning. And I left, like I said, either January or February 1940. So it was was quite a while.

speaker: interviewer
text: One more question. You say that you were to meet someone. Where was your mother and your sister at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- my sister-- my mother was the first one to leave. My father had arranged. I don't know how he managed. She got there. We had pre-arranged signal that she was there. We had lost our apartment, of course. We lived with somebody whose husband was taken to Dachau. So I was the next in line because Paula was staying with my grandmother, who was still in Vienna. She was the last one to go, but then it was already June-- no. It was-- I must have left-- I must have left in January '39, not '40, because Paula came in June 1939, two months before war broke out. So I had made it like January-February 1930, like I said. [? In ?] [? fact, ?] this time until the outbreak of war we lived in Poland and I remember my mother telling me-- now we were all together in one small apartment-- that people said, what a pity, such lovely young girls, the blonde one, my sisters both dark, and they can't speak and hear.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they felt so sorry for us. And it was such a strain not to speak. We hardly walked on the street. We were afraid. We-- we start to speak in German. And then when we got inside our apartment, we couldn't stop talking. It was a terrible thing. Besides, we had no papers. We were there illegally.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in Europe even today when you even go for a trip, you have to leave with the concierge your passport for registration with the police. Now, we didn't have any papers. My father had to bribe. He spent all the money. There was nothing left anymore-- to the caretaker not to denounce us. He knew that we were living there. And he always came, out-stretched hand, and wanted more and more and more.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me, why would you have to have play dumb and mute?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we didn't speak Polish. They would have thought we are Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what would happen if they would knew that you were Germans?

speaker: interviewee
text: We would have been in trouble because the-- the war was sort of brewing already. Poland lost after, I guess, a week, but they were enemies. Now here I come, an enemy. I don't even speak Polish. After my father who spoke perfectly Polish, he had not forgotten, neither my mother, but his two girls didn't. So it-- it would have been in dangering all of us had we spoke on the street German.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you live in-- in Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in the city of Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Lodz.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the city of Lodz. That brought all the unhappiness started in Lodz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe how it went from the day you arrived in Poland until you got out?

speaker: interviewee
text: As I said, it was a tremendous difficult time because we were afraid to go on the street. When the war started, September 1, 1939-- and a week later the Germans marched-- we watched it a second time the Germans marching into a city. But we could speak German. It wasn't a-- a problem anymore. But it wasn't a good-- they behaved just the way they did in Vienna and I suppose Berlin and Germany all over. Again we had to wear the Star of David. Again we were not allowed this or that. Only now a day, war was raging and they had-- nobody could restrain them. Nobody could hold them back. And only-- Poland is very cold. It's not Vancouver. In winter, it is so bitter, bitter cold. Yet, in February now we come to 1940. Two soldiers-- German soldiers came to our apartment. Eventually, they-- you know, they found out where the Jews live. Within five minutes, we had to leave our apartment. And I remember, like my mother said, please let me just get our papers. And I remember what he said, where you are going you don't need any papers, like documents. He was so right. We were marched into the ghetto of Lodz, which became known as the Lodz Ghetto, or translated, Litzmannstadt Ghetto. We were assigned one room. Not a one room apartment, one single room. It was a larger room and, you know, we didn't mind. We were-- besides, it wouldn't have helped. But the main thing was we were always concerned to be together. This was really-- that helped us over a lot of hurdles, being together. And May 1940, May 1, 1940, the ghetto was sealed off from the outside world. Barbed electric wire, guards everywhere, and nobody could go out of the ghetto and nobody could come into the ghetto. Only the German administration.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many people approximately, you think, it was in ghetto at the time when you were brought in?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 160,000 people, from the city of Lodz, and people like us and from other parts, and Polish Jews from surrounding places, more places maybe. Altogether 160,000. I don't tell you-- I don't know how it measures this, but it was far too many for such a small place.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me, was your father, mother, and your sister and you all together in that [INAUDIBLE]?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. That's what I told you. That's what made things bearable. Because we were together. The moment they had the doors closed to the ghetto, they appointed a man, a Jew, an older Jew, to be the administrator of the ghetto. His name was Chaim Rumkowski. He has gone down in history.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaim is C-H-A-I-M. Rumkowsi is R-U-M-K-O-W-S-K-I. And let me assure you, he ruled the ghetto with an iron fist because he said-- he kept having speeches all the time. And he kept telling us every time we assembled in the square that, if we work hard, if we do the best we can, we have a chance to survive. Now, workshops were established in the ghetto. That's what he referred to as work. Everybody had to work. If you didn't work, you didn't get a ration card. It was just as simple as that. It was a death sentence. Every imaginable kind of workshop, whatever's demanded in the state. Clothing, furniture, [? material ?], you know, [INAUDIBLE] everything. A bakery, a butcher-- I don't know about the butcher. We never had meat. Everything was done in this ghetto. And Chaim Rumkowski had this office in a certain place in the ghetto. And he couldn't leave either, but a German administrator-- his name was Hans-- H-A-N-S-- Biebow-- B-I-E-B-O-W. He came in with his helpers. All-- it's all Germans, of course. And he demanded-- Germany sent in raw products, and we worked 12 hours a day on a starvation diet, if I might let you know. It was one loaf of bread for a week, which sounds a lot today. I don't eat a loaf of bread a week, but it wasn't much more. In the beginning, we had potatoes, and it was a very highly-desired food. It shortly disappeared. Then we got, instead of potatoes, potato peels. They disappeared. And they were given out on doctor's prescription in the beginning, then it was taken off completely. We had a lot of red beets and kohlrabi. It's a vegetable. It's not the slightest [INAUDIBLE] of nourishment. And these workshops supplied [? the ?] soup. And then we came home and had another soup. I remember my mother making-- we had-- we didn't need ration cards to buy caraway seeds. My mother's favorite soup was caraway seeds. It gave it a color and a smell. And red beets were swimming around. I ate my kohlrabi raw, and we had some brown sugar. And that was my meat, my vegetable, my dessert. And we were hungry when we got up in the morning, and I can assure you, we were hungry when we go-- went to bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your job in that ghetto when you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because-- my mother [? in which ?] was German. Because I had worked shortly after I had graduated in a lawyer's office, I knew shorthand, which was then used, and of course I had perfect spelling and typing. Typing. Right. And that's why I had an office-- an office job. I worked in the-- the Jewish administration.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I could-- I was in constant touch with Rumkowski. I sat next to his office. He had his own private secretary. I wasn't a private secretary. I was just one of the girls. And I could see the Gestapo coming in and out, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I just lived in constant fear. Because I heard too much for my own good. But I had a job.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother worked in the factory where they made toys from scraps of wood that they produced in the factory where they made furniture. They made toys for the children in Germany, but ours starved to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: I have a question that is of a personal-- very personal nature. If you don't want to answer--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's fine.

speaker: interviewer
text: --please say so. If you want to answer in any way you like it, please do so. Your looks, as I saw in the pictures, are a woman of a blonde hair, blue eyes, which was appealing to the German people. I was Jewish. There was no appeal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Did they abuse you physically in any way? No. No. You know, Victoria, when they came to Vienna it started out as this. They declared that we are sub-humans. Not humans, sub-humans, that we are the scum of the earth. They were not interested in us as women. Maybe it happened in some places, incidence, no. No. Nobody looked at me. Absolutely not in any way than the bark out orders. That was it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you have stayed in that particular ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed in that ghetto for four years. And when I refer to hunger, let me tell you that, in four years in the ghetto, 40,000 people died of starvation.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you witnessed that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did. They were here today and gone tomorrow, like we say. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Before your eyes, they died. And then there were deportations. At one time, there were 200,000 people crammed into the ghetto. But no sooner had they come, they were deported.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they deported? Did you know where they were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. Today I know. They were killed in the gas chambers. But then you didn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to refer to 1942 especially. In 1942 I found out after the war was the year of the final solution declared. It was a conference in [? Buncie ?], referred to the [? Buncie ?] Conference. [? Buncie ?] is an elegant suburb of Berlin. People like Eichmann and Otto [? Heneckmann ?] sat at a long table and to discuss the destruction of Jews. Ovens, crematoria came in to being, gas chambers.

speaker: interviewee
text: And this was the year when all of a sudden the order came. Hans Biebow, the German administer, told Rumkowski-- I heard him, behind closed doors-- that 10,000 children, from babies to 10 years old, have to be deported. He had the audacity to ask-- not to ask-- to order Rumkowski to make lists of all the children in the ghetto. And Rumkowski stood on that marketplace. He was always watched by them. Always watched. And he said, this order has been given. In order to keep the ghetto alive, I have to ask you to give the best you have-- your children. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Now, anyone will understand, they can do whatever you want-- they want with you. They can not make you give up your child of your own free will. Nobody can do it. And what they said they'd do, they did. They said, if you don't succeed, we will come in with our trucks, and we will take the children. They ordered a curfew for the whole week. Nobody could go out and get their weekly rations. We were terribly, terribly hungry and even more scared. And they came in, and we had to-- everybody had to stand on the street [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] with their children. And I see it like it would happen yesterday. It is something nobody will ever forget. It's carved in our hearts. When they came and just took one after the other of those poor, defenseless children, their mothers jumping after them or their father, and loaded them onto the truck. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The truck came back empty in no time. Today I know. After the war, we knew there was gas in the truck. They didn't take the children far. They gassed them right in the truck. The curfew lasted for a-- one week. They took 11,000 of our children and 8,000 men and women over 50. And I will-- there was an incident. My heart was aching for every child. I was afraid to take my mother and father. There was one man-- I don't know his name. He was so-- we were all poor, but he did strike me that was poor all his life before the war even. His wife had died some time before. He had a little boy. Schmul was the little boy's name. He had brown curly hair and large blue eyes. And he was so thin, and he was so hungry, and he was holding onto his father's hand. And they took little Schmul, put him in the truck. And this poor-- he was a shoe maker-- a shoe maker. He stood there, and there was no little hand to hold on. I guess he had lost his mind. He didn't jump after little Schmul. And so little Schmul was killed with the other children. Mercifully, his father died two days later. I can't forget little Schmul. Together with so many children. That's one part of the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was in 1940?

speaker: interviewee
text: After it was over, the Gestapo calmly announced, we have to go. The stores are opened to get our ration, and we had to go immediately back to work. Does anyone understand what went on in our hearts? The ghetto was in mourning.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that when you worked in the office, you heard a lot, a lot more than was good for you. Can you tell us a little bit about it, what you have heard?

speaker: interviewee
text: I heard the pending deportation of the children. This was the most devastating blow. The children. And their other orders-- you know, there was forever, you know, threats. If you don't do this, this will happen. If you don't do that, that didn't happen. It was constantly the-- constantly the sword over Rumkowski's head because there are some people that say he sold out. I don't think so. I believe because of Rumkowski-- they would have done anyway. They didn't have to ask his permission. They were the masters. The ghetto wouldn't have existed, and I wouldn't be here talking. And I remember an incident. I watched many things. We had to work on Sundays in the office. We were skeleton staff, lest the Germans needed some urgent work to be done. And I was with Mary Schifflinger. It was the girl I was so closely associated with. It was a Sunday afternoon and Hans Biebow, the German administrator, stormed into the office. He was so drunk. And Rumkowski was in his office next to us. And wherever they came, we had to stand up and say--

speaker: interviewer
text: And salute?

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon, Herr [INAUDIBLE]. Good afternoon. And he stormed-- he dismissed us. He stormed into the office where Rumkowski sat. And it-- we had little iron stove stacks to keep us warm in the winter. And he marched in, and he grabbed Rumkowski's head and he hit it against that iron stove. I will never understand how he didn't break his head in pieces. And we had Jewish policemen. They noticed the incident because Mary and I, we had to stand still. I guess we forgot to breathe. We were so petrified. We couldn't go and help Rumkowski because he barked out an order, the German, [GERMAN]. The Jewish policemen alerted another German official, also from the administration that realized how drunk he was, and took Rumkowski away from his hands. He was all colors and he had to be hospitalized for quite a few days. But when that man, that German administrator, got into a drunken rage, it was hard. We were sure, the girl, Mary and I, that we will be the next one. But the other ones, they sort of took him away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that ghetto where you met your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: His name was Erich-- which is, here, like Eric-- Straus. I was inside not dead. I was hungry, but I was so determined to live. You know, my father every night when we assembled for that soup, he had the greatest hopes. He gave us always hope. And I thought he was the one that was most hungry because he was tall. And he said, there will come a day-- he said, I know there will come a day when there will be enough bread on the table, when there won't be borders, and we will be free.

speaker: interviewer
text: He was the one that never seen enough bread on the table, that never seen freedom. He was killed. But we all had that hope, that if we endure, if we try to cope with everything, we might. He always said, how long can the war last? It's got to end eventually, which made absolutely sense. We are going to stop now for changing the tape, and when we come back we will go to find out what's happened to your father, how he died, and also your marriage to your first husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I would like to say that.
